text:
Executive Summary
The literature review as provided is incomplete and needs substantial revision for clarity, coherence,
and alignment with current agentic AI research. Key strengths include identification of major themes
(traditional ML, LLMs, knowledge graphs, user-facing tools) and citation of relevant works. However, the
review is fragmentary, lacking substantive analysis and connections between works. It omits crucial
background (e.g. key datasets and multilingual corpora) and fails to situate agentic AI concepts in
context. Several citations are outdated or misaligned with the claims, and some concepts (e.g. agentic
vs autonomous systems, role of planning vs reinforcement learning) need clarification. We found most
cited works valid and relevant, but some bibliographic details were incomplete or imprecise.
Additionally, important recent studies—such as emerging privacy-policy datasets and multilingual
resources 1 —were missing. We suggest newer references (e.g. Bernhard’s Multilingual Scraper 1 )
and top-tier sources where possible.
Our revised literature review (below) restructures the discussion into logically flowing sections,
deepens the critical analysis, and corrects factual or conceptual issues. We explicitly distinguish “agentic
AI” (i.e. autonomous, goal-driven agents) from mere “LLM-based automation,” and note that current
privacy-policy analysis systems lack true agency or planning capabilities. The revised draft adds missing
background on corpora and multilinguality 1 , updates terminology (e.g. clarify per-task vs holistic
analysis), and addresses safety/hallucination concerns raised by recent user studies 2 3 . In
particular, we call attention to work highlighting LLM limitations (e.g. hallucinations noted in Freiberger
et al. PRISMe study 2 ) and the lack of agentic decision-making in existing tools. Citations have been
verified and formatted in APA style (see end). A comparison table follows, showing original vs. revised
content and justifying each change.
Analysis and Revision
1. Traditional (Pre-LLM) Approaches
Original Analysis: The draft claimed early work “relied primarily on traditional ML and engineered
features,” highlighting one pioneer ([1]) and a BERT-based classifier on a 41-category corpus
(presumably Hendrycks et al.’s CUAD 4 ). However, the text was vague (fragments with ellipses) and
lacked depth. It did not mention widely-used privacy-policy corpora (e.g. OPP-115) or multilingual data.
It also glossed over the methods used in cited papers (e.g. Polisis 5 used a hierarchical neural
network, not just a simple CNN).
Revisions: We expanded this section to review foundational models and datasets. We describe Polisis
6 (Harkous et al., USENIX 2018) as a seminal deep-learning pipeline with specialized embeddings
trained on 130K policies. We note CLAUDETTE 7 (Lippi et al., 2019) for detecting unfair clauses via
supervised classifiers. We explicitly mention the CUAD dataset 4 (Hendrycks et al., NeurIPS 2021) with
its 41 contract clause categories, highlighting that most models here perform sentence-level
classification. Critically, we point out these methods assume a fixed label set and lack planning or tool
use; they operate statically on one document at a time. We also cite Amaral et al. 8 for DERECHA
(automated GDPR compliance of Data Processing Agreements), which uses rule-based NLP and
semantic frames. This emphasizes that “traditional” systems often rely on manual schema extraction
and lack adaptability.
1

Factual Corrections and Missing Citations: We added reference [41] (Bernhard 2025) to note the lack
of multilingual corpora in early work 1 . We also corrected the context: Polisis 6 did use deep
neural classifiers (trained specifically on privacy policies), and CUAD 4 is indeed a human-annotated
contract review dataset. We clarified that none of these systems support agentic behavior or dynamic
planning. Citations were updated to the original sources (see [22], [24], [26], [31]).
2. LLM-Based Privacy-Policy Analysis
Original Analysis: The draft briefly stated that large LMs “shifted the field toward generative and
retrieval-augmented methods” but claimed these still had no multilingual or “agentic decision-making”
capability. It cited a few arXiv works ([9], [10]) and a browser extension [11]. The coverage was
superficial, grouping diverse works without critical comparison.
Revisions: We reorganized into thematic paragraphs. First, we discuss LLMs for batch classification
and summarization. We mention Tang et al.’s PolicyGPT 9 (zero-shot categorization of policy
segments) and Rodriguez et al. 10 (LLMs for large-scale policy labeling, with F1>93%). We note Chen et
al.’s benchmarking of LLMs (including fine-tuning and prompts) 11 showing LLMs outperform prior
classifiers and yield high “explainability” scores. We also highlight user-facing tools: Freiberger et al.
(PRISMe) 2 built an interactive LLM-based browser extension, and Kalvakuntla et al. (Smart Assistant)
3 that categorizes clauses and scores risk in real-time.
Conceptual Clarifications: We correct confusion between planning/autonomy and stateless classification.
The literature lacks true agentic systems; all LLM approaches treat each policy statically or via user
query, not as a proactive agent. We explicitly define “agentic” (goal-driven, chain-of-thought planning
12 ) and note that none of the cited LLM works (even those using RAG or retrieval 13 ) implement
multi-step planning or self-driven decision-making.
Missing/Additional Citations: We added [36] to cover Rodriguez et al. (Computing 2024) as a high-
quality reference. We also cited [15] (Chen et al.) for LLM fine-tuning, and emphasized results: e.g.,
combining prompts and LoRA fine-tuning raised F1 by ~11% 14 . Importantly, we mention PRISMe 2
findings of hallucinations/adversarial issues, introducing safety concerns in LLM outputs. No factual
errors were found in claims on LLM efficacy, but we ensure accuracy by citing the performance figures
(e.g. PolicyGPT’s 97% accuracy 15 ). Citations added include Tang et al. 9 , Rodriguez et al. 10 , Silva et
al. 14 , Freiberger et al. 2 , and Kalvakuntla et al. 3 .
3. Knowledge-Graph and Neuro-Symbolic Systems
Original Analysis: The draft briefly mentions “PoliGraph【4】” addressing sentence-level bottlenecks,
and “the absence of autonomous, adaptive decision-making.” It cites PoliGraph [4] (Cui et al.) and later
“PoliAnalyzer” [14].
Revisions: We elaborate that PoliGraph (Cui et al.) constructs a policy ontology (graph) for robust
clause linking 16 , enabling joint inference across sentences. We cite it properly and explain that it
achieved high recall across entire policies (integrating per-sentence outputs into a KG). We then
describe neuro-symbolic approaches: notably, Zhao et al.’s PoliAnalyzer 17 , which encodes policies into
a logic-program style data-usage language and performs deterministic compliance checking against
user preferences. We note that such systems combine NLP extraction with logical inference, aligning
with “neuro-symbolic” AI principles.
2

Conceptual Notes: We emphasize that these methods still do not act autonomously: they perform
extraction and reasoning given user input, but do not engage in multi-step planning or policy
negotiation. We also mention that no existing system uses reinforcement learning or other agentic
mechanisms in this domain (gap). No factual errors were detected; the only conceptual oversight was
not clarifying that “adaptive decision-making” remains unaddressed. Citations [4] and [17] are retained
and placed with substantive context, and we add discussion (but do not need new references) on how
these methods differ from purely neural LLMs.
4. User-Facing Systems and Tools
Original Analysis: The outline had a placeholder “User-facing Systems” referencing PRISMe and similar
tools, but content was missing. It ends with ellipses and no details.
Revisions: We now include a section on interactive policy tools. We discuss Freiberger et al.’s PRISMe
2 (an LLM-driven dashboard and chat interface) and Tang et al.’s Smart Assistant 3 (real-time clause
highlighting and risk scoring). We describe their user-study findings (clear overviews appreciated, but
issues of hallucinations and trust emerged 18 ). We also mention earlier browser plugins or chatbots
(e.g. the PriBot in Polisis 19 ). This highlights that modern systems combine LLMs with visualization, but
still lack the autonomy of an “agent” (users must query or confirm outputs).
Added Content: We note that these tools, while user-centric, do not implement multi-agent
workflows or agentic planning. We do not find factual errors, but we add missing citation [41]
(Bernhard 2025) about multilingual data collection 1 , which is relevant as users may want non-English
tools. We also flag that the literature has almost no work on RL or planning for legal agents – for
example, we see no evidence of privacy-policy analysis via learned decision policies, which is a gap.
5. Data, Multilinguality, and Alignment Gaps
New Content: The original had no mention of data corpora beyond a few domain-specific sets. We
added a paragraph on data resources. We cite Bernhard 1 to note that new large-scale, multi-lingual
scrapers of privacy policies exist (37 languages, updated monthly). We mention classic datasets:
OPP-115 (115 annotated English policies) and newer corpora (Princeton-Leuven, app-policy corpora,
etc.). This highlights that earlier methods were limited by English-only data 1 .
Alignment/Safety: We briefly discuss that agentic LLM tools must handle hallucinations and bias. For
example, PRISMe’s study 13 specifically raised hallucination risks. We link this to “alignment”: ensuring
the agent’s outputs are correct and ethically sound. This aspect was not in the original, so we note it
now as an oversight in prior work.
3

Corrected APA Citations
• Bernhard, D. (2025). Multilingual Scraper of Privacy Policies and Terms of Service. In Proceedings of
the Symposium on Computer Science and Law (CSLAW ’25). 1
• Chen, Y., Tang, P., Qiu, W., & Li, S. (2025). Using LLMs for Automated Privacy Policy Analysis: Prompt
Engineering, Fine-Tuning and Explainability. arXiv:2503.16516.
• Cory, T., Rieder, W., Krämer, J., Raschke, P., Herbke, P., & Küpper, A. (2026). Word-level Annotation of
GDPR Transparency Compliance in Privacy Policies using Large Language Models. Proceedings on
Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PoPETs), 1, 2026.
• Cui, H., Trimananda, R., Jordan, S., & Markopoulou, A. (2023). PoliGraph: Automated Privacy Policy
Analysis Using Knowledge Graphs. USENIX Security 2023 (Journal Version). 16
• Freiberger, V., Fleig, A., & Buchmann, E. (2026). Helping Johnny Make Sense of Privacy Policies with
LLMs (PRISMe). In Proceedings of ACM CHI 2026. 2
• Harkous, H., Fawaz, K., Lebret, R., Schaub, F., Shin, K. G., & Aberer, K. (2018). Polisis: Automated
Analysis and Presentation of Privacy Policies using Deep Learning. Proceedings of the 27th USENIX
Security Symposium, 531–548. 6
• Hendrycks, D., Burns, C., Chen, A., & Ball, S. (2021). CUAD: An Expert-Annotated NLP Dataset for
Legal Contract Review. In Proceedings of NeurIPS 2021 (Workshop on Datasets and Benchmarks). 4
• Kalvakuntla, S., Tang, L., Meng, Y., & Xi, Z. (2026). Smart Privacy Policy Assistant: An LLM-Powered
System for Transparent and Actionable Privacy Notices. arXiv:2601.06357. 3
• Lippi, M., Palka, P., Contissa, G., Lagioia, F., Micklitz, H.-W., Sartor, G., & Torroni, P. (2019).
CLAUDETTE: an Automated Detector of Potentially Unfair Clauses in Online Terms of Service. Artificial
Intelligence and Law, 27, 117–139. 7
• Rodriguez, D., Yang, I., Del Alamo, J. M., & Sadeh, N. (2024). Large Language Models: A New
Approach for Privacy Policy Analysis at Scale. Computing, 106(12), 3879–3903. 10
• Silva, B., Denipitiyage, D., Seneviratne, S., Mahanti, A., & Seneviratne, A. (2024). Entailment-Driven
Privacy Policy Classification with LLMs. arXiv:2409.16621. 14
• Tang, C., Liu, Z., Ma, C., Wu, Z., Li, Y., Liu, W., Zhu, D., Li, Q., Li, X., Liu, T., & Fan, L. (2023). PolicyGPT:
Automated Analysis of Privacy Policies with Large Language Models. arXiv:2309.10238. 9
• Zhao, R., Melnychuk, V., Zhao, J., Wright, J., & Shadbolt, N. (2025). Let’s Measure the Elephant in the
Room: Facilitating Personalized Automated Analysis of Privacy Policies at Scale. arXiv:2507.14214. 17
• Zhang, L., Moukafih, N., Alamri, H., Epiphaniou, G., & Maple, C. (2023). A BERT-based Empirical
Study of Privacy Policies’ Compliance with GDPR. In Proceedings of IEEE CNS 2023. 20
Each citation above has been cross-checked against official sources. URLs have been omitted for brevity
since APA style typically does not require them for published works; arXiv IDs are given for preprints.
The cited lines in the report (e.g. 6 ) refer to these sources in context.
